segments.text: It's 11:47 PM in your Queens apartment. Your body is tired, but your mind won't clock out. You replay one text and one awkward pause like your brain is hunting danger before tomorrow starts.
Overthinking feels productive, but it's mostly threat-scanning. In New York, everything moves fast, so your nervous system treats every silence and delay like an emergency.
You call it preparation. But your shoulders rise, your jaw tightens, your breathing gets shallow, and you time-travel into worst-case futures that haven't happened.
Try this reset now: feet on the floor, name five things you can see, loosen your hands, and exhale longer than you inhale. This tells your body you're here, not in imagined disaster.
Then give your mind one container: write your top three worries and one next action for each. Not tonight. Tomorrow. Your brain can stop looping when it sees a plan.
You are not broken because you think deeply. You're overloaded. Gen Z carries pressure from work, money, and nonstop feeds. The goal isn't to never think, it's to stop spiraling.
Tonight, use one sentence: I can handle tomorrow tomorrow. Say it once. Breathe out. You don't need perfect certainty to rest, only one small moment of safety.
